message.content:
Research how to grant macOS Accessibility permissions to a specific Node.js script rather than the entire node binary. The user wants to:

1. NOT give accessibility permissions to WebStorm or the global node binary
2. Have a way to authorize just the compliance CLI tool

Explore these approaches:
1. Creating a standalone macOS .app bundle that wraps the Node script
2. Using tools like `pkg`, `nexe`, or `bun build --compile` to create a standalone binary
3. Creating a simple shell wrapper that could be authorized
4. Any other macOS-specific solutions (like creating a signed binary)

Focus on practical solutions that would work for this use case. Check if bun has native compilation support that could help.

{
  "name": "belt",
  "version": "0.1.2",
  "private": true,
  "description": "TypeScript utility library with runtime type validation, state management, and common helpers",
  "repository": {
    "type": "git",
    "url": "https://github.com/antoniopresto/belt"
  },
  "author": "@antoniopresto <antoniopresto@gmail.com>",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.3.5",
  "workspaces": [
    "packages/*"
  ],
  "engines": {
    "node": ">=20.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "build": "turbo run build",
    "test": "turbo run test",
    "check": "turbo run check",
    "format": "prettier ./prettier.config.mjs",
    "publish": "run-s publish:*",
    "publish:patch": "bun run version:patch",
    "publish:push": "git push origin main --tags",
    "version:patch": "./scripts/bump-version.sh patch",
    "version:minor": "./scripts/bump-version.sh minor",
    "version:major": "./scripts/bump-version.sh major"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@belt/prettier-plugin": "latest",
    "@types/node": "^25.0.3",
    "npm-run-all": "^4.1.5",
    "prettier": "^3.7.4",
    "prettier-plugin-organize-imports": "^4.3.0",
    "turbo": "^2.7.2",
    "typescript": "^5.9.3"
  }
}

Based on the search results, here's information about using pkg and nexe for converting Node.js applications to standalone binaries on macOS:

## Overview of pkg and nexe

Nexe is a command-line utility that compiles your Node.js application into a single executable file. Similarly, pkg is a command-line tool for Node.js that allows you to package your Node.js project into an executable that can be run even on machines without Node.js installed.

## Current Status

It's worth noting that nexe has had no releases since 2017 and therefore no support for newer versions of Node.js and is considered unmaintained at this point in time. This makes **pkg** the more actively maintained and recommended option for modern Node.js development.

## Key Benefits

Both tools provide similar functionality:

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - codebytere/node-mac-permissions: A native node module to manage system permissions on macOS.","url":"https://github.com/codebytere/node-mac-permissions"},{"title":"macos-accessibility-permissions - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/macos-accessibility-permissions/v/1.0.0"},{"title":"node-mac-permissions - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/node-mac-permissions"},{"title":"node-mac-permissions/README.md at main · codebytere/node-mac-permissions","url":"https://github.com/codebytere/node-mac-permissions/blob/main/README.md"},{"title":"@nut-tree-fork/node-mac-permissions - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/@nut-tree-fork/node-mac-permissions"},{"title":"GitHub - ahkohd/macos_accessibility_client: A Node.JS Wrapper around macOS' accessibility client APIs","url":"https://github.com/ahkohd/macos_accessibility_client"},{"title":"node-mac-permissions/permissions.mm at main · codebytere/node-mac-permissions","url":"https://github.com/codebytere/node-mac-permissions/blob/main/permissions.mm"},{"title":"Solve “EACCES: permission denied” Error in Node.js on macOS | by Harshil Moradia | Medium","url":"https://harshilmoradia.medium.com/solve-eacces-permission-denied-error-in-node-js-on-macos-ce7e48bd2fa3"},{"title":"How to fix EACCES errors with NPM on MacOS","url":"https://www.willandskill.se/nyheter/how-to-fix-eacces-errors-with-npm-on-macos"},{"title":"GitHub - mmaietta/node-mac-permissions: A native node module to manage system permissions on macOS.","url":"https://github.com/mmaietta/node-mac-permissions"}]

Based on the search results, I found information relevant to macOS accessibility permissions and Node.js:

## Key Findings:

**Node.js Module for macOS Permissions:**
The `node-mac-permissions` is a native node module to manage system permissions on macOS. This module can handle accessibility permissions among other system permissions.

**Accessibility Permission Support:**
The module supports various permission types including accessibility, bluetooth, calendar, camera, contacts, full-disk-access, input-monitoring, location, microphone, notifications, photos, reminders, screen, and speech-recognition.

**Accessibility Permission Status:**
The module can check authorization status which can return one of not determined, denied, authorized, limited, provisional, or restricted.

**Important Limitation:**
There is no API for programmatically requesting Accessibility access on macOS at this time, and so calling this method will trigger opening of System Preferences at the Accessibility pane of Security and Privacy.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about `bun build --compile` for creating native binaries on macOS:

## Building Native Binaries with Bun

Bun's bundler implements a --compile flag for generating a standalone binary from a TypeScript or JavaScript file.(no content)All imported files and packages are bundled into the executable, along with a copy of the Bun runtime.

## macOS-Specific Considerations

To codesign a standalone executable on macOS (which fixes Gatekeeper warnings), use the codesign command. This is the closest information in the search results related to macOS and security considerations for compiled binaries.

## Cross-Compilation Support

Here are the search results for granting Accessibility permissions to specific macOS applications:

## Graphical Method

When a third-party app tries to access and control your Mac through accessibility features, you must specifically grant the app access to your Mac in Privacy & Security settings. The process involves:

1. Go to System Settings, click Privacy & Security in the sidebar, then click Accessibility, and turn permission on or off for any app in the list.

2.

Here are the search results for granting Accessibility permissions to specific macOS applications:

## Graphical Method

When a third-party app tries to access and control your Mac through accessibility features, you must specifically grant the app access to your Mac in Privacy & Security settings. The process involves:

1. Go to System Settings, click Privacy & Security in the sidebar, then click Accessibility, and turn permission on or off for any app in the list.
